trajectory.description: Observation: Previous training produced NaN loss from batch 0, leading to model learning nothing and macro_f1=0.0. val_macro_f1 was 0.002, worse than random. Analysis: NaN loss could be caused by class weights, mixed precision, or DataParallel issues. Decision: Simplify training: remove class weights, disable mixed precision, add anomaly detection and NaN checks, use gradient clipping, and start with a very small learning rate (1e-5). Train on 10% data for 3 epochs to get loss finite and a meaningful macro_f1 score. Then submit eval to measure improvement and iteration time.

This will give a reliable baseline and iteration time measurement.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iter 11 failed with shape mismatch (16x768 vs 512x182) from model.py, and train.py was incomplete/truncated. Analysis: The classifier dimension was mismatched with the vision encoder output, likely due to model_name changes or hardcoded dimensions. Decision: Rewrite model.py to auto-detect output dim via dummy forward, rewrite train.py completely with DataParallel, class weights, label smoothing, warmup, cosine LR, saving best checkpoint, and logging. Use CLIP ViT-L/14 for stronger feature extraction. Also update predict.py to load model_name from checkpoint for robust loading. Train for 3 epochs on full data (est. ~3h) to get baseline and time measurement, then submit eval.

Observation: Iter 12 achieved macro_f1=0.0799 (significantly improved from 0.0099) using CLIP ViT-B/16 fine-tuned for 3 epochs. Analysis: The linear classifier head on top of frozen features may be suboptimal for long-tailed classification. A stronger head (MLP with class weights) trained on frozen features can boost macro_f1, and this can be done quickly within remaining 1.1h. Decision: Extract features from the trained model, train a 2-layer MLP head with class weights, label smoothing, and feature-space mixup, replace classifier in checkpoint, and submit eval.

Analysis: Submitting best_head.pt may yield higher test macro_f1 than the 0.0799 from full fine-tuning. However, predict.py currently uses a linear classifier; head training might have used an MLP, causing mismatch and wrong predictions. Decision: Rewrite predict.py to auto-detect classifier architecture from the checkpoint (handles linear or multi-layer MLP), load vision encoder from pretrained, and load weights with strict alignment. Submit eval immediately with best_head.pt to get final score before time runs out.

trajectory.command:
python -c "
import torch
# Build ensemble checkpoint from existing models
checkpoints = ['checkpoints/best.pt', 'checkpoints/best_head.pt']
models = []
for path in checkpoints:
    ckpt = torch.load(path, map_location='cpu')
    if 'model_state_dict' in ckpt:
        sd = ckpt['model_state_dict']
        mn = ckpt.get('model_name', None)
    elif 'state_dict' in ckpt:
        sd = ckpt['state_dict']
        mn = ckpt.get('model_name', None)
    else:
        sd = ckpt
        mn = None
    if mn is None:
        stripped = {k[7:] if k.startswith('module.') else k: v for k, v in sd.items()}
        if 'classifier.0.weight' in stripped:
            in_dim = stripped['classifier.0.weight'].shape[1]
            mn = 'openai/clip-vit-large-patch14' if in_dim == 1024 else 'openai/clip-vit-base-patch16'
        elif 'classifier.weight' in stripped:
            in_dim = stripped['classifier.weight'].shape[1]
            mn = 'openai/clip-vit-large-patch14' if in_dim == 1024 else 'openai/clip-vit-base-patch16'
        else:
            mn = 'openai/clip-vit-base-patch16'
    models.append((sd, mn))
ensemble = {'ensemble': True, 'models': models}
torch.save(ensemble, 'checkpoints/ensemble.pt')
print('Ensemble checkpoint created with', len(models), 'models')
# Quick validation: load with predict.py on CPU
from predict import load_ensemble_models
mods = load_ensemble_models('checkpoints/ensemble.pt', num_classes=182, device='cpu')
print('Successfully loaded ensemble of', len(mods), 'models')
"
trajectory.description: Observation: macro_f1 improved to 0.1621 using MLP head on frozen ViT-L features. Time left 0.54h. Analysis: Ensemble of ViT-B (0.0799) and ViT-L (0.1621) models can leverage complementary features to boost OOD generalization, feasible within remaining time (inference ~10 min). Decision: Create an ensemble checkpoint combining both full models, update predict.py to support multi-model averaging, and submit eval with the ensemble. This is the safest short-term improvement.
